title: One Nation malcontents surf the RBA's inflation wave
full_text:
Monetary policy Until the Reserve Bank gets on top of inflation, voter frustration about the cost of living is likely to persist and One Nation will thrive.
The shock rise of Pauline Hanson's One Nation can plausibly be attributed to a range of factors related to economics, national identity and broader cultural issues.
Almost one in three voters say they would vote for One Nation at a federal election, according to recent polls. Consecutive federal and state governments that failed to lift living standards, out-of-touch elite institutions and over-the-top progressive activists should all share blame.
But the Reserve Bank of Australia cannot shirk its role in contributing to the feeling of economic grievance in the community.
Persistent high inflation and cost-of-living pressures have indisputably contributed to voter discontent with the major political parties. Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said about One Nation's rise last month, "It's the economy, stupid." That is, the economy Labor has presided over for four years.
The underlying inflation rate - which largely strips out temporary government subsidy gimmicks - has been above the RBA's 2.5 per cent midpoint target since late 2021.
High federal and state government spending and a lack of pro-productivity policies from multiple governments have contributed to price pressures. The average price level of goods and services is almost 25 per cent higher in Australia than in early 2020, before COVID-19 and government-mandated lockdowns hit.
As an aside on the pandemic, the severe overreaction by governments to lock down the community and deny basic freedoms to citizens for years helped sow the seeds of discontent among One Nation supporters.
Locking up children and denying them an in-school education and socialisation also appear to be factors behind the terrifying youth crime in Victoria, playing into One Nation's tough-on-crime and hardline immigration agenda.
But I digress. The RBA cannot get off scot-free. Underlying inflation, or so-called trimmed mean inflation, will be above the target point for a total of almost seven years until mid-2028, according to RBA forecasts.
As the bank's governor Michele Bullock has said, everyone feels the impact of high inflation, particularly vulnerable people in our community. High inflation renders people poorer by making things more expensive, reducing real (inflation-adjusted) incomes and eroding purchasing power.
New research by the e61 Institute's Gianni La Cava shows a strong link between high inflation and weak consumer sentiment.
"When inflation stays elevated, its effect accumulates, consistent with households reacting to the cost of a sustained rise in prices," notes La Cava, a former RBA economist.
Voters are pessimistic about their financial future. A recent voter poll in The Sydney Morning Herald and The Age found only a quarter of Australians expected economic conditions to improve in the next year, while 44 per cent thought things would get worse.
Despite all the bleating by politicians and tabloid media about higher interest rates hurting households, the e61 research shows mortgage rates are much less of a concern than inflation.
"Household survey data show similar falls in financial satisfaction across mortgagors, outright owners and renters, consistent with a general cost-of-living shock rather than a mortgage-specific channel," La Cava says.
"The evidence suggests it is the persistent price pressures, rather than mortgage rates, which are driving the pessimism across all household types, suggesting a return to confidence depends on inflation easing back to the target band and real incomes having time to rebuild."
The RBA's three interest rate cuts in 2025, admittedly with some benefit of hindsight, were a mistake and prolonged the inflation battle. The pressure from government MPs and Labor-aligned economists before the last election for the RBA to cut rates was an error that helped stoke resurgent inflation.
The RBA has been forced to backflip with three interest rate rises this year and is keeping open the possibility of further monetary policy tightening.
Yet, for much of the previous five years, the RBA's interest rate-setting board has been complacent on inflation and instead prioritised maintaining full employment.
The unemployment rate is a low 4.4 per cent. But headline inflation is 4 per cent and underlying inflation is 3.6 per cent, well above target.
Inflation was above target before the conflict in Iran erupted in late February and pushed up fuel prices.
The RBA's full-employment strategy follows the Albanese government's independent review of the RBA, pressure from Treasury and lobbying by Labor-aligned economists for jobs to be given more consideration in the RBA's dual mandate of inflation and full employment.
What this strategy didn't account for was the political economy ramifications of persistently above-target inflation.
In promoting this strategy, the RBA board, Treasury and the government didn't factor in the political gift they were giving Hanson. High inflation and cost-of-living pressures have made voters grumpy and more willing to turn to political outsiders and away from the established major parties.
The complacency on inflation didn't consider other negative consequences, such as increased industrial action by unions in response to rising prices and falling real wages.
This week, I received an email from my daughter's Canberra primary school advising that because the ACT government had not agreed to an above-inflation pay rise, there would be no written comments on student reports and no parent-teacher interviews. ACT educators had a full strike day in early June - a rehash of the COVID-era denial of children's education and socialisation.
The ironically named "education" union - "teacher" union would be more appropriate - has rejected the offer of a 9 per cent pay rise over three years and is citing a 32 per cent pay rise over four years that Victorian teachers secured.
Some 35,000 educators in Victoria walked off the job for one day in March to demand they receive a large pay rise to counter inflation.
Now, there are tentative signs the RBA has woken up to its errors and is more wary of inflation.
At her June 16 press conference after holding interest rates steady, Bullock explained that keeping interest rates too low to artificially stimulate employment while inflation remains high would be counterproductive.
Failing to tame inflation would ultimately cause much worse damage to the jobs market in the long run because it would mean interest rates would need to rise even higher to crush inflation expectations.
"Unless we can get inflation down, then ultimately it's going to lead to higher unemployment," Bullock said.
Until the RBA sustainably gets on top of inflation, voter frustration about the cost of living is likely to persist and One Nation can surf the wave of malcontents.
John Kehoe is The Australian Financial Review's economics editor.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: John Kehoe
heading: Banking and Economy
